---
title: "MapCountry"
author: "alanlhutchison"
date: "2025-08-05"
output: html_document
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = TRUE)
```

## R Markdown

This is an R Markdown document. Markdown is a simple formatting syntax for authoring HTML, PDF, and MS Word documents. For more details on using R Markdown see <http://rmarkdown.rstudio.com>.

When you click the **Knit** button a document will be generated that includes both content as well as the output of any embedded R code chunks within the document. You can embed an R code chunk like this:

```{r }
# Load the necessary libraries
library(ggplot2)
library(dplyr)
library(rnaturalearth)
library(rnaturalearthdata)

# 1. Read in the transplant data using tribble()
transplant_data <- tribble(
  ~country_origin, ~type,      ~transplants, ~rnaturalearth_name,
  "BAHRAIN",       "Deceased", 1,            "Bahrain",
  "BAHRAIN",       "Living",   0,            "Bahrain",
  "BERMUDA",       "Deceased", 1,            "Bermuda",
  "BERMUDA",       "Living",   0,            "Bermuda",
  "BRAZIL",        "Deceased", 1,            "Brazil",
  "BRAZIL",        "Living",   0,            "Brazil",
  "BURUNDI",       "Deceased", 1,            "Burundi",
  "BURUNDI",       "Living",   0,            "Burundi",
  "CAMBODIA",      "Deceased", 1,            "Cambodia",
  "CAMBODIA",      "Living",   0,            "Cambodia",
  "CANADA",        "Deceased", 4,            "Canada",
  "CANADA",        "Living",   0,            "Canada",
  "CAYMAN ISLAND", "Deceased", 2,            "Cayman Islands",
  "CAYMAN ISLAND", "Living",   0,            "Cayman Islands",
  "CHILE",         "Deceased", 1,            "Chile",
  "CHILE",         "Living",   0,            "Chile",
  "COSTA RICA",    "Deceased", 1,            "Costa Rica",
  "COSTA RICA",    "Living",   0,            "Costa Rica",
  "CUBA",          "Deceased", 1,            "Cuba",
  "CUBA",          "Living",   0,            "Cuba",
  "DOMINICAN REPUBLIC", "Deceased", 3,       "Dominican Republic",
  "DOMINICAN REPUBLIC", "Living",   1,       "Dominican Republic",
  "EGYPT",         "Deceased", 2,            "Egypt",
  "EGYPT",         "Living",   0,            "Egypt",
  "EL SALVADOR",   "Deceased", 4,            "El Salvador",
  "EL SALVADOR",   "Living",   0,            "El Salvador",
  "GREECE",        "Deceased", 2,            "Greece",
  "GREECE",        "Living",   0,            "Greece",
  "GUATEMALA",     "Deceased", 1,            "Guatemala",
  "GUATEMALA",     "Living",   0,            "Guatemala",
  "ISRAEL",        "Deceased", 38,           "Israel",
  "ISRAEL",        "Living",   0,            "Israel",
  "JAPAN",         "Deceased", 1,            "Japan",
  "JAPAN",         "Living",   0,            "Japan",
  "JORDAN",        "Deceased", 2,            "Jordan",
  "JORDAN",        "Living",   13,           "Jordan",
  "KUWAIT",        "Deceased", 31,           "Kuwait",
  "KUWAIT",        "Living",   14,           "Kuwait",
  "LEBANON",       "Deceased", 1,            "Lebanon",
  "LEBANON",       "Living",   0,            "Lebanon",
  "MEXICO",        "Deceased", 1,            "Mexico",
  "MEXICO",        "Living",   0,            "Mexico",
  "NICARAGUA",     "Deceased", 1,            "Nicaragua",
  "NICARAGUA",     "Living",   0,            "Nicaragua",
  "PARAGUAY",      "Deceased", 1,            "Paraguay",
  "PARAGUAY",      "Living",   0,            "Paraguay",
  "PHILIPPINES",   "Deceased", 4,            "Philippines",
  "PHILIPPINES",   "Living",   1,            "Philippines",
  "QATAR",         "Deceased", 1,            "Qatar",
  "QATAR",         "Living",   1,            "Qatar",
  "RUSSIA",        "Deceased", 17,           "Russia",
  "RUSSIA",        "Living",   4,            "Russia",
  "SAUDI ARABIA",  "Deceased", 62,           "Saudi Arabia",
  "SAUDI ARABIA",  "Living",   7,            "Saudi Arabia",
  "SINGAPORE",     "Deceased", 1,            "Singapore",
  "SINGAPORE",     "Living",   0,            "Singapore",
  "SOUTH AFRICA",  "Deceased", 1,            "South Africa",
  "SOUTH AFRICA",  "Living",   0,            "South Africa",
  "TURKEY",        "Deceased", 12,           "Turkey",
  "TURKEY",        "Living",   6,            "Turkey",
  "UNITED ARAB EMIRATES", "Deceased", 6,     "United Arab Emirates",
  "UNITED ARAB EMIRATES", "Living",   1,     "United Arab Emirates",
  "UNITED KINGDOM", "Deceased", 3,          "United Kingdom",
  "UNITED KINGDOM", "Living",   0,          "United Kingdom",
  "VENEZUELA",     "Deceased", 4,            "Venezuela",
  "VENEZUELA",     "Living",   1,            "Venezuela",
  "VIET NAM",      "Deceased", 3,            "Viet Nam",
  "VIET NAM",      "Living",   0,            "Viet Nam",
  "YEMEN:REPUBLIC OF", "Deceased", 1,       "Yemen",
  "YEMEN:REPUBLIC OF", "Living",   0,       "Yemen"
)

# 2. Get the world map data
world_map <- ne_countries(scale = "medium", returnclass = "sf")

# 3. Create a template for all countries and both types
# This ensures that every country has a 'type' value.
map_data_template <- world_map %>% 
  select(name_long, geometry) %>% 
  # Use full_join to make sure every country is in the template
  full_join(
    expand.grid(name_long = unique(world_map$name_long), 
                type = c("Deceased", "Living")),
    by = "name_long"
  )

# 4. Merge the template with the transplant data
# Now, we join the cleaned transplant data back to the template.
world_map_with_data <- map_data_template %>%
  left_join(transplant_data %>% select(rnaturalearth_name, type, transplants), 
            by = c("name_long" = "rnaturalearth_name", "type")) %>%
  # Fill NA values for transplants with 0 for countries with no data
  mutate(transplants = tidyr::replace_na(transplants, 0))

# 5. Create the two faceted maps
ggplot(data = world_map_with_data) +
  geom_sf(aes(fill = transplants)) +
  
  # Set a consistent color scale for fair comparison
  scale_fill_continuous(
    option = "magma", direction = -1,
    name = "Number of Transplants", 
    na.value = "grey80", 
    limits = c(0, max(transplant_data$transplants, na.rm = TRUE)) 
  ) +
  
  # Use facet_wrap to create a separate map for each 'type' (Deceased/Living)
  facet_wrap(~ type,ncol=1) +
  
  # Polish the maps for a clean look
  labs(
    title = "Deceased vs. Living Transplants by Country",
    subtitle = "Data from a provided bar chart"
  ) +
  theme_void() +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(hjust = 0.5, face = "bold", size = 16),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(hjust = 0.5),
    legend.position = "bottom",
    strip.text = element_text(face = "bold", size = 12)
  )
```


```{r}

# Option to suppress the warning if you want a clean console
centroids_sf <- suppressWarnings(
  st_centroid(world_map, of_largest_geom = TRUE)
) %>%
  st_transform(crs = "+proj=longlat +datum=WGS84")

# Load the necessary libraries
library(ggplot2)
library(dplyr)
library(rnaturalearth)
library(rnaturalearthdata)
library(sf)
library(tidyr) # The expand_grid function is in tidyr

# 1. Read in the transplant data
transplant_data <- tribble(
  ~country_origin, ~type,      ~transplants, ~rnaturalearth_name,
  "BAHRAIN",       "Deceased", 1,            "Bahrain",
  "BAHRAIN",       "Living",   0,            "Bahrain",
  "BERMUDA",       "Deceased", 1,            "Bermuda",
  "BERMUDA",       "Living",   0,            "Bermuda",
  "BRAZIL",        "Deceased", 1,            "Brazil",
  "BRAZIL",        "Living",   0,            "Brazil",
  "BURUNDI",       "Deceased", 1,            "Burundi",
  "BURUNDI",       "Living",   0,            "Burundi",
  "CAMBODIA",      "Deceased", 1,            "Cambodia",
  "CAMBODIA",      "Living",   0,            "Cambodia",
  "CANADA",        "Deceased", 4,            "Canada",
  "CANADA",        "Living",   0,            "Canada",
  "CAYMAN ISLAND", "Deceased", 2,            "Cayman Islands",
  "CAYMAN ISLAND", "Living",   0,            "Cayman Islands",
  "CHILE",         "Deceased", 1,            "Chile",
  "CHILE",         "Living",   0,            "Chile",
  "COSTA RICA",    "Deceased", 1,            "Costa Rica",
  "COSTA RICA",    "Living",   0,            "Costa Rica",
  "CUBA",          "Deceased", 1,            "Cuba",
  "CUBA",          "Living",   0,            "Cuba",
  "DOMINICAN REPUBLIC", "Deceased", 3,       "Dominican Republic",
  "DOMINICAN REPUBLIC", "Living",   1,       "Dominican Republic",
  "EGYPT",         "Deceased", 2,            "Egypt",
  "EGYPT",         "Living",   0,            "Egypt",
  "EL SALVADOR",   "Deceased", 4,            "El Salvador",
  "EL SALVADOR",   "Living",   0,            "El Salvador",
  "GREECE",        "Deceased", 2,            "Greece",
  "GREECE",        "Living",   0,            "Greece",
  "GUATEMALA",     "Deceased", 1,            "Guatemala",
  "GUATEMALA",     "Living",   0,            "Guatemala",
  "ISRAEL",        "Deceased", 38,           "Israel",
  "ISRAEL",        "Living",   0,            "Israel",
  "JAPAN",         "Deceased", 1,            "Japan",
  "JAPAN",         "Living",   0,            "Japan",
  "JORDAN",        "Deceased", 2,            "Jordan",
  "JORDAN",        "Living",   13,           "Jordan",
  "KUWAIT",        "Deceased", 31,           "Kuwait",
  "KUWAIT",        "Living",   14,           "Kuwait",
  "LEBANON",       "Deceased", 1,            "Lebanon",
  "LEBANON",       "Living",   0,            "Lebanon",
  "MEXICO",        "Deceased", 1,            "Mexico",
  "MEXICO",        "Living",   0,            "Mexico",
  "NICARAGUA",     "Deceased", 1,            "Nicaragua",
  "NICARAGUA",     "Living",   0,            "Nicaragua",
  "PARAGUAY",      "Deceased", 1,            "Paraguay",
  "PARAGUAY",      "Living",   0,            "Paraguay",
  "PHILIPPINES",   "Deceased", 4,            "Philippines",
  "PHILIPPINES",   "Living",   1,            "Philippines",
  "QATAR",         "Deceased", 1,            "Qatar",
  "QATAR",         "Living",   1,            "Qatar",
  "RUSSIA",        "Deceased", 17,           "Russia",
  "RUSSIA",        "Living",   4,            "Russia",
  "SAUDI ARABIA",  "Deceased", 62,           "Saudi Arabia",
  "SAUDI ARABIA",  "Living",   7,            "Saudi Arabia",
  "SINGAPORE",     "Deceased", 1,            "Singapore",
  "SINGAPORE",     "Living",   0,            "Singapore",
  "SOUTH AFRICA",  "Deceased", 1,            "South Africa",
  "SOUTH AFRICA",  "Living",   0,            "South Africa",
  "TURKEY",        "Deceased", 12,           "Turkey",
  "TURKEY",         "Living",   6,            "Turkey",
  "UNITED ARAB EMIRATES", "Deceased", 6,     "United Arab Emirates",
  "UNITED ARAB EMIRATES", "Living",   1,     "United Arab Emirates",
  "UNITED KINGDOM", "Deceased", 3,          "United Kingdom",
  "UNITED KINGDOM", "Living",   0,          "United Kingdom",
  "VENEZUELA",     "Deceased", 4,            "Venezuela",
  "VENEZUELA",     "Living",   1,            "Venezuela",
  "VIET NAM",      "Deceased", 3,            "Viet Nam",
  "VIET NAM",      "Living",   0,            "Viet Nam",
  "YEMEN:REPUBLIC OF", "Deceased", 1,       "Yemen",
  "YEMEN:REPUBLIC OF", "Living",   0,       "Yemen"
)

# 2. Get the world map data
world_map <- ne_countries(scale = "medium", returnclass = "sf")

# 3. Create a clean, non-spatial data frame with all the information
# This is the key step. We build the full data set first.
full_plot_data <- tibble(name_long = unique(world_map$name_long)) %>%
  tidyr::expand_grid(type = c("Deceased", "Living")) %>%
  left_join(transplant_data, by = c("name_long" = "rnaturalearth_name", "type")) %>%
  mutate(transplants = tidyr::replace_na(transplants, 0))

# 4. Compute centroids and join the clean data to the spatial object
# We join the spatial data with the non-spatial data
centroids_sf <- st_centroid(world_map, of_largest_geom = TRUE) %>%
  st_transform(crs = "+proj=longlat +datum=WGS84")

# Now, we join the full non-spatial data to the centroids
# The 'type' column is in full_plot_data, and we want to keep all rows from it
centroid_data_for_plot <- centroids_sf %>%
  right_join(full_plot_data, by = "name_long") %>%
  # Filter for only countries with transplants for the dots
  filter(transplants > 0)

# 5. Create the two faceted proportional symbol maps
ggplot() +
  # Layer 1: Plot the country borders as a background
  geom_sf(data = world_map, fill = "grey95", color = "white", size = 0.2) +

  # Layer 2: Plot the circles at the centroids
  geom_sf(
    data = centroid_data_for_plot,
    aes(size = transplants),
    shape = 21, # Circle with border
    color = "black",
    alpha = 0.7
  ) +

  # Set the scale for the circle sizes
  scale_size_continuous(range = c(1, 10), name = "Number of Transplants") +

  # Set the colors for the deceased and living transplants
  scale_fill_manual(
    values = c("Deceased" = "firebrick3", "Living" = "royalblue3"),
    name = "Transplant Type"
  ) +

  # Use facet_wrap to create a separate map for each 'type'
  #facet_wrap(~ type) +

  # Add polish
  labs(
    title = "Proportional Symbol Map of Transplants by Country",
    subtitle = "Size of circle is proportional to the number of transplants"
  ) +
  theme_void() +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(hjust = 0.5, face = "bold", size = 16),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(hjust = 0.5),
    legend.position = "bottom",
    strip.text = element_text(face = "bold", size = 12)
  )


```


```{r}
# Install necessary packages if you haven't already
# install.packages("rworldmap")
# install.packages("cartogram")
# install.packages("sf") # sf is often a dependency for spatial data

# Load the required libraries
library(rworldmap)
library(cartogram)
library(sf)
library(ggplot2) # For better plotting if desired, though cartogram has its own plot method

# --- 1. Get World Map Data ---
# Get the base world map data. rworldmap provides a convenient way to do this.
# We'll convert it to an 'sf' object, which is preferred by the 'cartogram' package.
world_map_sf <- st_as_sf(getMap(resolution = "low"))

# --- IMPORTANT FIX: Project the map data ---
# The cartogram functions work best with projected data, especially for area calculations.
# We'll transform the map to an equal-area projection (e.g., World Equal Area Cylindrical - EPSG:8857).
# This ensures that area distortions are calculated correctly.
cat("Transforming map projection...\n")
world_map_projected <- st_transform(world_map_sf, crs = 8857) # EPSG:8857 for World Equal Area Cylindrical
cat("Map projection transformed.\n")

# --- 2. Create Sample Data ---
# Create a data frame with country names (matching 'ISO_A2' or 'SOVEREIGNT' in world_map_sf)
# and some integer values.
# Replace this with your actual table data.
# Ensure your country identifiers match those in the 'world_map_sf' object.
# Common identifiers are 'ISO_A3' (3-letter ISO code), 'ISO_A2' (2-letter ISO code),
# or 'SOVEREIGNT' (sovereign country name). Let's use 'SOVEREIGNT' for simplicity here.

sample_data <- data.frame(
  country = c("United States", "China", "India", "Germany", "Brazil", "Canada",
              "Australia", "France", "Japan", "United Kingdom", "Russia", "Egypt",
              "South Africa", "Argentina", "Mexico", "Indonesia", "Italy", "Spain",
              "Nigeria", "Ethiopia", "Greenland", "Antarctica", "Somalia", "Finland",
              "New Zealand", "Madagascar", "Chile", "Norway", "Sweden", "Poland",
              "Ukraine", "Kazakhstan", "Saudi Arabia", "South Korea", "North Korea",
              "Iran", "Iraq", "Afghanistan", "Pakistan", "Turkey", "Greece",
              "Portugal", "Ireland", "Switzerland", "Austria", "Belgium",
              "Netherlands", "Denmark", "Czech Republic", "Hungary", "Romania",
              "Bulgaria", "Serbia", "Croatia", "Bosnia and Herzegovina", "Albania",
              "Kosovo", "North Macedonia", "Slovenia", "Slovakia", "Estonia",
              "Latvia", "Lithuania", "Belarus", "Moldova", "Syria", "Lebanon",
              "Israel", "Jordan", "Yemen", "Oman", "United Arab Emirates",
              "Qatar", "Bahrain", "Kuwait", "Azerbaijan", "Armenia", "Georgia",
              "Uzbekistan", "Turkmenistan", "Kyrgyzstan", "Tajikistan", "Mongolia",
              "Nepal", "Bhutan", "Bangladesh", "Myanmar", "Thailand", "Vietnam",
              "Cambodia", "Laos", "Malaysia", "Singapore", "Philippines",
              "Sri Lanka", "Maldives", "Papua New Guinea", "Fiji", "Solomon Islands",
              "Vanuatu", "New Caledonia", "East Timor", "Brunei", "Timor-Leste",
              "Indonesia", "Australia", "New Zealand", "Fiji", "Samoa", "Tonga",
              "Kiribati", "Tuvalu", "Nauru", "Palau", "Micronesia", "Marshall Islands",
              "Canada", "United States", "Mexico", "Guatemala", "Belize", "El Salvador",
              "Honduras", "Nicaragua", "Costa Rica", "Panama", "Cuba", "Jamaica",
              "Haiti", "Dominican Republic", "Puerto Rico", "Bahamas", "Trinidad and Tobago",
              "Barbados", "Grenada", "Saint Lucia", "Saint Vincent and the Grenadines",
              "Antigua and Barbuda", "Saint Kitts and Nevis", "Dominica", "Venezuela",
              "Colombia", "Ecuador", "Peru", "Bolivia", "Paraguay", "Uruguay",
              "Argentina", "Chile", "Guyana", "Suriname", "French Guiana", "Brazil",
              "United Kingdom", "Ireland", "France", "Spain", "Portugal", "Italy",
              "Belgium", "Netherlands", "Luxembourg", "Germany", "Switzerland",
              "Austria", "Czech Republic", "Poland", "Hungary", "Slovakia",
              "Slovenia", "Croatia", "Bosnia and Herzegovina", "Serbia", "Montenegro",
              "Albania", "North Macedonia", "Greece", "Bulgaria", "Romania",
              "Moldova", "Ukraine", "Belarus", "Lithuania", "Latvia", "Estonia",
              "Finland", "Sweden", "Norway", "Denmark", "Iceland", "Russia",
              "Turkey", "Cyprus", "Malta", "Egypt", "Libya", "Tunisia", "Algeria",
              "Morocco", "Mauritania", "Western Sahara", "Senegal", "Gambia",
              "Guinea-Bissau", "Guinea", "Sierra Leone", "Liberia", "Ivory Coast",
              "Ghana", "Togo", "Benin", "Nigeria", "Niger", "Burkina Faso", "Mali",
              "Chad", "Cameroon", "Central African Republic", "Sudan", "South Sudan",
              "Eritrea", "Djibouti", "Ethiopia", "Somalia", "Kenya", "Uganda",
              "Rwanda", "Burundi", "Tanzania", "Democratic Republic of the Congo",
              "Republic of the Congo", "Gabon", "Equatorial Guinea", "Sao Tome and Principe",
              "Angola", "Zambia", "Malawi", "Mozambique", "Zimbabwe", "Botswana",
              "Namibia", "South Africa", "Lesotho", "Eswatini", "Madagascar",
              "Comoros", "Mauritius", "Seychelles"
              ),
  value = c(5000,rep(1,247)) # Random values for demonstration
)


# --- 3. Merge Sample Data with Map Data ---
# It's crucial that the country names in your sample_data match a column in world_map_projected.
# We'll use 'SOVEREIGNT' from world_map_projected and 'country' from sample_data for merging.
# First, ensure both are character strings for proper merging.
world_map_projected$SOVEREIGNT <- as.character(world_map_projected$SOVEREIGNT)
sample_data$country <- as.character(sample_data$country)

# Merge the data. Use a left join to keep all map polygons.
# Countries in world_map_projected that are not in sample_data will have NA for 'value'.
world_map_with_data <- merge(
  world_map_projected, # Use the projected map data here
  sample_data,
  by.x = "SOVEREIGNT", # Column in world_map_projected
  by.y = "country",    # Column in sample_data
  all.x = TRUE         # Keep all rows from world_map_projected
)

# Handle NA values: For countries not in your sample_data, their 'value' will be NA.
# The cartogram function might struggle with NAs or treat them as 0.
# It's good practice to decide how to handle them. For this example, let's set NA values to 0.
world_map_with_data$value[is.na(world_map_with_data$value)] <- 0

# --- 4. Create the Cartogram ---
# Note: Cartogram generation can be computationally intensive for very detailed maps
# or very large datasets.

cat("Generating cartogram... This might take a moment.\n")

# Option 1: Continuous Area Cartogram (distorts shapes)
# This will distort the actual shapes of the countries.
cartogram_result <- cartogram_cont(
  world_map_with_data,
  weight = "value", # The column containing your integer values
  itermax = 100      # Increased iterations for potentially more visible distortion
)

# Option 2: Dorling Cartogram (uses circles, often more visually striking for size scaling)
# This will represent each country as a circle scaled by its 'value'.
# cartogram_result <- cartogram_dorling(
#   world_map_with_data,
#   weight = "value", # The column containing your integer values
#   k = 1,            # Scaling factor for circle size (adjust as needed)
#   itermax = 100     # More iterations for better packing of circles
# )

cat("Cartogram generation complete.\n")

# --- 5. Plot the Cartogram ---
# You can plot the 'sf' object directly.
# Let's color the countries based on the 'value' for better visualization.

# Determine the number of colors in the palette
num_colors <- 10
color_palette <- hcl.colors(num_colors, "viridis")

# Calculate breaks: need one more break than colors
# Ensure breaks span the range of your 'value' data
min_val <- min(cartogram_result$value, na.rm = TRUE)
max_val <- max(cartogram_result$value, na.rm = TRUE)
plot_breaks <- seq(min_val, max_val, length.out = num_colors + 1)


# Plotting with base plot function for sf objects
plot(
  cartogram_result["value"], # Plot the 'value' column
  main = "World Map Scaled by Sample Value (Continuous Cartogram)", # Updated title
  key.pos = 4, # Position of the legend (1=bottom, 2=left, 3=top, 4=right)
  pal = color_palette, # Color palette
  breaks = plot_breaks, # Explicitly define breaks
  border = "grey", # Border color for countries
  lwd = 0.5 # Line width for borders
)

# Add original map for comparison (optional)
# plot(st_geometry(world_map_projected), border = "blue", add = TRUE) # This would overlay

# If you prefer ggplot2 for more customization:
# library(ggplot2)
# ggplot(data = cartogram_result) +
#   geom_sf(aes(fill = value), color = "grey", size = 0.2) +
#   scale_fill_viridis_c(option = "plasma", name = "Your Value") +
#   labs(title = "World Map Scaled by Sample Value (Cartogram)") +
#   theme_minimal() +
#   theme(
#     plot.title = element_text(hjust = 0.5, face = "bold"),
#     legend.position = "right"
#   )

# You can also plot the original map to compare
# plot(world_map_projected["value"], main = "Original World Map with Sample Value")

```
```{r}
# Install necessary packages if you haven't already
# install.packages("rworldmap")
# install.packages("cartogram")
# install.packages("sf") # sf is often a dependency for spatial data

# Load the required libraries
library(rworldmap)
library(cartogram)
library(sf)
library(ggplot2) # For better plotting if desired, though cartogram has its own plot method

# --- 1. Get World Map Data ---
# Get the base world map data. rworldmap provides a convenient way to do this.
# We'll convert it to an 'sf' object, which is preferred by the 'cartogram' package.
world_map_sf <- st_as_sf(getMap(resolution = "low"))

# --- IMPORTANT FIX: Project the map data ---
# The cartogram functions work best with projected data, especially for area calculations.
# We'll transform the map to an equal-area projection (e.g., World Equal Area Cylindrical - EPSG:8857).
# This ensures that area distortions are calculated correctly.
cat("Transforming map projection...\n")
world_map_projected <- st_transform(world_map_sf, crs = 8857) # EPSG:8857 for World Equal Area Cylindrical
cat("Map projection transformed.\n")

# --- 2. Create Sample Data ---
# Create a data frame with country names (matching 'ISO_A2' or 'SOVEREIGNT' in world_map_sf)
# and some integer values.
# Replace this with your actual table data.
# Ensure your country identifiers match those in the 'world_map_sf' object.
# Common identifiers are 'ISO_A3' (3-letter ISO code), 'ISO_A2' (2-letter ISO code),
# or 'SOVEREIGNT' (sovereign country name). Let's use 'SOVEREIGNT' for simplicity here.

sample_data <- data.frame(
  country = c("United States", "China", "India", "Germany", "Brazil", "Canada",
              "Australia", "France", "Japan", "United Kingdom", "Russia", "Egypt",
              "South Africa", "Argentina", "Mexico", "Indonesia", "Italy", "Spain",
              "Nigeria", "Ethiopia", "Greenland", "Antarctica", "Somalia", "Finland",
              "New Zealand", "Madagascar", "Chile", "Norway", "Sweden", "Poland",
              "Ukraine", "Kazakhstan", "Saudi Arabia", "South Korea", "North Korea",
              "Iran", "Iraq", "Afghanistan", "Pakistan", "Turkey", "Greece",
              "Portugal", "Ireland", "Switzerland", "Austria", "Belgium",
              "Netherlands", "Denmark", "Czech Republic", "Hungary", "Romania",
              "Bulgaria", "Serbia", "Croatia", "Bosnia and Herzegovina", "Albania",
              "Kosovo", "North Macedonia", "Slovenia", "Slovakia", "Estonia",
              "Latvia", "Lithuania", "Belarus", "Moldova", "Syria", "Lebanon",
              "Israel", "Jordan", "Yemen", "Oman", "United Arab Emirates",
              "Qatar", "Bahrain", "Kuwait", "Azerbaijan", "Armenia", "Georgia",
              "Uzbekistan", "Turkmenistan", "Kyrgyzstan", "Tajikistan", "Mongolia",
              "Nepal", "Bhutan", "Bangladesh", "Myanmar", "Thailand", "Vietnam",
              "Cambodia", "Laos", "Malaysia", "Singapore", "Philippines",
              "Sri Lanka", "Maldives", "Papua New Guinea", "Fiji", "Solomon Islands",
              "Vanuatu", "New Caledonia", "East Timor", "Brunei", "Timor-Leste",
              "Indonesia", "Australia", "New Zealand", "Fiji", "Samoa", "Tonga",
              "Kiribati", "Tuvalu", "Nauru", "Palau", "Micronesia", "Marshall Islands",
              "Canada", "United States", "Mexico", "Guatemala", "Belize", "El Salvador",
              "Honduras", "Nicaragua", "Costa Rica", "Panama", "Cuba", "Jamaica",
              "Haiti", "Dominican Republic", "Puerto Rico", "Bahamas", "Trinidad and Tobago",
              "Barbados", "Grenada", "Saint Lucia", "Saint Vincent and the Grenadines",
              "Antigua and Barbuda", "Saint Kitts and Nevis", "Dominica", "Venezuela",
              "Colombia", "Ecuador", "Peru", "Bolivia", "Paraguay", "Uruguay",
              "Argentina", "Chile", "Guyana", "Suriname", "French Guiana", "Brazil",
              "United Kingdom", "Ireland", "France", "Spain", "Portugal", "Italy",
              "Belgium", "Netherlands", "Luxembourg", "Germany", "Switzerland",
              "Austria", "Czech Republic", "Poland", "Hungary", "Slovakia",
              "Slovenia", "Croatia", "Bosnia and Herzegovina", "Serbia", "Montenegro",
              "Albania", "North Macedonia", "Greece", "Bulgaria", "Romania",
              "Moldova", "Ukraine", "Belarus", "Lithuania", "Latvia", "Estonia",
              "Finland", "Sweden", "Norway", "Denmark", "Iceland", "Russia",
              "Turkey", "Cyprus", "Malta", "Egypt", "Libya", "Tunisia", "Algeria",
              "Morocco", "Mauritania", "Western Sahara", "Senegal", "Gambia",
              "Guinea-Bissau", "Guinea", "Sierra Leone", "Liberia", "Ivory Coast",
              "Ghana", "Togo", "Benin", "Nigeria", "Niger", "Burkina Faso", "Mali",
              "Chad", "Cameroon", "Central African Republic", "Sudan", "South Sudan",
              "Eritrea", "Djibouti", "Ethiopia", "Somalia", "Kenya", "Uganda",
              "Rwanda", "Burundi", "Tanzania", "Democratic Republic of the Congo",
              "Republic of the Congo", "Gabon", "Equatorial Guinea", "Sao Tome and Principe",
              "Angola", "Zambia", "Malawi", "Mozambique", "Zimbabwe", "Botswana",
              "Namibia", "South Africa", "Lesotho", "Eswatini", "Madagascar",
              "Comoros", "Mauritius", "Seychelles"
              ),
  value = sample(1:248, 248, replace = TRUE) # Random values for demonstration
)


# --- 3. Merge Sample Data with Map Data ---
# It's crucial that the country names in your sample_data match a column in world_map_projected.
# We'll use 'SOVEREIGNT' from world_map_projected and 'country' from sample_data for merging.
# First, ensure both are character strings for proper merging.
world_map_projected$SOVEREIGNT <- as.character(world_map_projected$SOVEREIGNT)
sample_data$country <- as.character(sample_data$country)

# Merge the data. Use a left join to keep all map polygons.
# Countries in world_map_projected that are not in sample_data will have NA for 'value'.
world_map_with_data <- merge(
  world_map_projected, # Use the projected map data here
  sample_data,
  by.x = "SOVEREIGNT", # Column in world_map_projected
  by.y = "country",    # Column in sample_data
  all.x = TRUE         # Keep all rows from world_map_projected
)

# Handle NA values: For countries not in your sample_data, their 'value' will be NA.
# The cartogram function might struggle with NAs or treat them as 0.
# It's good practice to decide how to handle them. For this example, let's set NA values to 0.
world_map_with_data$value[is.na(world_map_with_data$value)] <- 0

# --- 4. Create the Cartogram ---
# Note: Cartogram generation can be computationally intensive for very detailed maps
# or very large datasets.

cat("Generating cartogram... This might take a moment.\n")

# Option 1: Continuous Area Cartogram (distorts shapes)
# This will distort the actual shapes of the countries.
cartogram_result <- cartogram_cont(
  world_map_with_data,
  weight = "value", # The column containing your integer values
  itermax = 10      # Increased iterations for potentially more visible distortion
)

# Option 2: Dorling Cartogram (uses circles, often more visually striking for size scaling)
# This will represent each country as a circle scaled by its 'value'.
# cartogram_result <- cartogram_dorling(
#   world_map_with_data,
#   weight = "value", # The column containing your integer values
#   k = 1,            # Scaling factor for circle size (adjust as needed)
#   itermax = 100     # More iterations for better packing of circles
# )

cat("Cartogram generation complete.\n")

# --- 5. Plot the Cartogram ---
# You can plot the 'sf' object directly.
# Let's color the countries based on the 'value' for better visualization.

# Determine the number of colors in the palette
num_colors <- 10
color_palette <- hcl.colors(num_colors, "viridis")

# Calculate breaks: need one more break than colors
# Ensure breaks span the range of your 'value' data
min_val <- min(cartogram_result$value, na.rm = TRUE)
max_val <- max(cartogram_result$value, na.rm = TRUE)
plot_breaks <- seq(min_val, max_val, length.out = num_colors + 1)


# Plotting with base plot function for sf objects
plot(
  cartogram_result["value"], # Plot the 'value' column
  main = "World Map Scaled by Sample Value (Continuous Cartogram)", # Updated title
  key.pos = 4, # Position of the legend (1=bottom, 2=left, 3=top, 4=right)
  pal = color_palette, # Color palette
  breaks = plot_breaks, # Explicitly define breaks
  border = "grey", # Border color for countries
  lwd = 0.5 # Line width for borders
)

# Add original map for comparison (optional)
# plot(st_geometry(world_map_projected), border = "blue", add = TRUE) # This would overlay

# If you prefer ggplot2 for more customization:
# library(ggplot2)
# ggplot(data = cartogram_result) +
#   geom_sf(aes(fill = value), color = "grey", size = 0.2) +
#   scale_fill_viridis_c(option = "plasma", name = "Your Value") +
#   labs(title = "World Map Scaled by Sample Value (Cartogram)") +
#   theme_minimal() +
#   theme(
#     plot.title = element_text(hjust = 0.5, face = "bold"),
#     legend.position = "right"
#   )

# You can also plot the original map to compare
# plot(world_map_projected["value"], main = "Original World Map with Sample Value")

```



```{r}
# Install necessary packages if you haven't already
# install.packages("rworldmap")
# install.packages("cartogram")
# install.packages("sf") # sf is often a dependency for spatial data

# Load the required libraries
library(rworldmap)
library(cartogram)
library(sf)
library(ggplot2) # For better plotting if desired, though cartogram has its own plot method

# --- 1. Get World Map Data ---
# Get the base world map data. rworldmap provides a convenient way to do this.
# We'll convert it to an 'sf' object, which is preferred by the 'cartogram' package.
world_map_sf <- st_as_sf(getMap(resolution = "low"))

# --- IMPORTANT FIX: Project the map data ---
# The cartogram functions work best with projected data, especially for area calculations.
# We'll transform the map to an equal-area projection (e.g., World Equal Area Cylindrical - EPSG:8857).
# This ensures that area distortions are calculated correctly.
cat("Transforming map projection...\n")
world_map_projected <- st_transform(world_map_sf, crs = 8857) # EPSG:8857 for World Equal Area Cylindrical
cat("Map projection transformed.\n")

# --- 2. Create Sample Data ---
# Create a data frame with country names and integer values.
# I've chosen a mix of countries with very high, medium, and very low values
# to make the distortion more apparent. Countries not listed here will effectively
# have a value of 0 and will be filtered out.
# Replace this with your actual table data.
# Ensure your country identifiers match those in the 'world_map_sf' object.
# 'SOVEREIGNT' is used here for merging.

sample_data <- data.frame(
  country = c("United States", "China", "India", "Germany", "Brazil", "Canada",
              "Australia", "France", "Japan", "United Kingdom", "Russia", "Nigeria",
              "Egypt", "South Africa", "Argentina", "Mexico", "Indonesia", "Italy",
              "Spain", "Greenland", "Somalia", "Finland", "New Zealand",
              "Myanmar", "Thailand", "Vietnam", "Saudi Arabia", "Iran", "Turkey",
              "Poland", "Ukraine", "Kazakhstan", "Algeria", "Democratic Republic of the Congo",
              "Belgium", "Netherlands", "Switzerland", "Luxembourg", "Singapore", "Iceland"
              ),
  value = c(500, 600, 450, 150, 200, 100, # Very High/High values for large countries
            120, 180, 220, 140, 350, 80,  # Medium values
            60, 70, 100, 170, 280, 130,
            110, 10, 20, 30, 40,       # Low values for smaller or less populated countries
            90, 100, 110, 160, 170, 180,
            120, 130, 100, 50, 40,
            55, 65, 75, 5, 80, 15 # Very low values for small countries to show shrinkage
            )
)


# --- 3. Merge Sample Data with Map Data ---
# It's crucial that the country names in your sample_data match a column in world_map_projected.
# We'll use 'SOVEREIGNT' from world_map_projected and 'country' from sample_data for merging.
# First, ensure both are character strings for proper merging.
world_map_projected$SOVEREIGNT <- as.character(world_map_projected$SOVEREIGNT)
sample_data$country <- as.character(sample_data$country)

# Merge the data. Use a left join to keep all map polygons.
# Countries in world_map_projected that are not in sample_data will have NA for 'value'.
world_map_with_data <- merge(
  world_map_projected, # Use the projected map data here
  sample_data,
  by.x = "SOVEREIGNT", # Column in world_map_projected
  by.y = "country",    # Column in sample_data
  all.x = TRUE         # Keep all rows from world_map_projected
)

# Handle NA values: For countries not in your sample_data, their 'value' will be NA.
# Set NA values to 0 so we can filter them out.
world_map_with_data$value[is.na(world_map_with_data$value)] <- 0

# --- IMPORTANT: Filter out countries with zero value ---
# This ensures the cartogram only processes and displays countries with actual data.
cat("Filtering countries with zero values...\n")
world_map_filtered <- world_map_with_data[world_map_with_data$value > 0, ]
cat(paste0("Filtered map contains ", nrow(world_map_filtered), " countries.\n"))


# --- 4. Create the Cartogram ---
# Note: Cartogram generation can be computationally intensive for very detailed maps
# or very large datasets.

cat("Generating continuous cartogram... This might take a moment.\n")

# Using Continuous Area Cartogram (distorts shapes)
# Increased iterations to allow for more distortion, but there are inherent limits
# due to shape and contiguity preservation.
cartogram_result <- cartogram_cont(
  world_map_filtered, # Use the filtered data here
  weight = "value", # The column containing your integer values
  itermax = 50      # Increased iterations for potentially more visible distortion
)

cat("Continuous cartogram generation complete.\n")

# --- 5. Plot the Cartogram ---
# You can plot the 'sf' object directly.
# Let's color the countries based on the 'value' for better visualization.

# Determine the number of colors in the palette
num_colors <- 10
color_palette <- hcl.colors(num_colors, "viridis")

# Calculate breaks: need one more break than colors
# Ensure breaks span the range of your 'value' data
min_val <- min(cartogram_result$value, na.rm = TRUE)
max_val <- max(cartogram_result$value, na.rm = TRUE)
plot_breaks <- seq(min_val, max_val, length.out = num_colors + 1)


# Plotting with base plot function for sf objects
plot(
  cartogram_result["value"], # Plot the 'value' column
  main = "World Map Scaled by Sample Value (Continuous Cartogram)", # Updated title
  key.pos = 4, # Position of the legend (1=bottom, 2=left, 3=top, 4=right)
  pal = color_palette, # Color palette
  breaks = plot_breaks, # Explicitly define breaks
  border = "grey", # Border color for countries
  lwd = 0.5 # Line width for borders
)

# You can also plot the original map for comparison (optional)
# plot(st_geometry(world_map_filtered), border = "blue", add = TRUE) # This would overlay

```


```{r}
# Load the necessary libraries
library(ggplot2)
library(dplyr)
library(rnaturalearth)
library(rnaturalearthdata)
library(sf) # Needed for spatial operations like calculating centroids
library(tidyr) # For replace_na
library(ggrepel) # NEW: For non-overlapping labels

# 1. Read in the transplant data using tribble()
# This data contains the number of deceased and living transplants for various countries.
transplant_data <- tribble(
  ~country_origin, ~type,       ~transplants, ~rnaturalearth_name,
  "BAHRAIN",       "Deceased", 1,             "Bahrain",
  "BAHRAIN",       "Living",    0,             "Bahrain",
  "BERMUDA",       "Deceased", 1,             "Bermuda",
  "BERMUDA",       "Living",    0,             "Bermuda",
  "BRAZIL",        "Deceased", 1,             "Brazil",
  "BRAZIL",        "Living",    0,             "Brazil",
  "BURUNDI",       "Deceased", 1,             "Burundi",
  "BURUNDI",       "Living",    0,             "Burundi",
  "CAMBODIA",      "Deceased", 1,             "Cambodia",
  "CAMBODIA",      "Living",    0,             "Cambodia",
  "CANADA",        "Deceased", 4,             "Canada",
  "CANADA",        "Living",    0,             "Canada",
  "CAYMAN ISLAND", "Deceased", 2,             "Cayman Islands",
  "CAYMAN ISLAND", "Living",    0,             "Cayman Islands",
  "CHILE",         "Deceased", 1,             "Chile",
  "CHILE",         "Living",    0,             "Chile",
  "COSTA RICA",    "Deceased", 1,             "Costa Rica",
  "COSTA RICA",    "Living",    0,             "Costa Rica",
  "CUBA",          "Deceased", 1,             "Cuba",
  "CUBA",          "Living",    0,             "Cuba",
  "DOMINICAN REPUBLIC", "Deceased", 3,         "Dominican Republic",
  "DOMINICAN REPUBLIC", "Living",    1,         "Dominican Republic",
  "EGYPT",         "Deceased", 2,             "Egypt",
  "EGYPT",         "Living",    0,             "Egypt",
  "EL SALVADOR",   "Deceased", 4,             "El Salvador",
  "EL SALVADOR",   "Living",    0,             "El Salvador",
  "GREECE",        "Deceased", 2,             "Greece",
  "GREECE",        "Living",    0,             "Greece",
  "GUATEMALA",     "Deceased", 1,             "Guatemala",
  "GUATEMALA",     "Living",    0,             "Guatemala",
  "ISRAEL",        "Deceased", 38,            "Israel",
  "ISRAEL",        "Living",    0,             "Israel",
  "JAPAN",         "Deceased", 1,             "Japan",
  "JAPAN",         "Living",    0,             "Japan",
  "JORDAN",        "Deceased", 2,             "Jordan",
  "JORDAN",        "Living",    13,            "Jordan",
  "KUWAIT",        "Deceased", 31,            "Kuwait",
  "KUWAIT",        "Living",    14,            "Kuwait",
  "LEBANON",       "Deceased", 1,             "Lebanon",
  "LEBANON",       "Living",    0,             "Lebanon",
  "MEXICO",        "Deceased", 1,             "Mexico",
  "MEXICO",        "Living",    0,             "Mexico",
  "NICARAGUA",     "Deceased", 1,             "Nicaragua",
  "NICARAGUA",     "Living",    0,             "Nicaragua",
  "PARAGUAY",      "Deceased", 1,             "Paraguay",
  "PARAGUAY",      "Living",    0,             "Paraguay",
  "PHILIPPINES",   "Deceased", 4,             "Philippines",
  "PHILIPPINES",   "Living",    1,             "Philippines",
  "QATAR",         "Deceased", 1,             "Qatar",
  "QATAR",         "Living",    1,             "Qatar",
  "RUSSIA",        "Deceased", 17,            "Russia",
  "RUSSIA",        "Living",    4,             "Russia",
  "SAUDI ARABIA",  "Deceased", 62,            "Saudi Arabia",
  "SAUDI ARABIA",  "Living",    7,             "Saudi Arabia",
  "SINGAPORE",     "Deceased", 1,             "Singapore",
  "SINGAPORE",     "Living",    0,             "Singapore",
  "SOUTH AFRICA",  "Deceased", 1,             "South Africa",
  "SOUTH AFRICA",  "Living",    0,             "South Africa",
  "TURKEY",        "Deceased", 12,            "Turkey",
  "TURKEY",        "Living",    6,             "Turkey",
  "UNITED ARAB EMIRATES", "Deceased", 6,       "United Arab Emirates",
  "UNITED ARAB EMIRATES", "Living",    1,       "United Arab Emirates",
  "UNITED KINGDOM", "Deceased", 3,            "United Kingdom",
  "UNITED KINGDOM", "Living",    0,            "United Kingdom",
  "VENEZUELA",     "Deceased", 4,             "Venezuela",
  "VENEZUELA",     "Living",    1,             "Venezuela",
  "VIET NAM",      "Deceased", 3,             "Viet Nam",
  "VIET NAM",      "Living",    0,             "Viet Nam",
  "YEMEN:REPUBLIC OF", "Deceased", 1,         "Yemen",
  "YEMEN:REPUBLIC OF", "Living",    0,         "Yemen"
)

# 2. Get the world map data
# We use 'ne_countries' from 'rnaturalearth' to get geographic data for countries.
world_map <- ne_countries(scale = "medium", returnclass = "sf")

# --- Calculate centroids for all countries in the base map ---
# To place numbers at the center of each country, we need their geographic centroids.
# st_centroid() calculates the centroid of each country's polygon.
# st_coordinates() extracts the longitude (X) and latitude (Y) from these centroids.
country_centroids <- world_map %>%
  st_centroid() %>%
  st_coordinates() %>%
  as_tibble() %>% # Convert to a tibble (data frame)
  rename(lon = X, lat = Y) %>% # Rename columns for clarity
  bind_cols(name_long = world_map$name_long) # Add country names to link back to map data

# 3. Create a template for all countries and both types
# This ensures that every country is considered for both "Deceased" and "Living" transplant types,
# even if they don't have data for one or both.
map_data_template <- world_map %>%
  select(name_long, geometry) %>% # Select only the country name and its geographic shape
  full_join(
    expand.grid(name_long = unique(world_map$name_long), # Create all combinations of countries and types
                type = c("Deceased", "Living")),
    by = "name_long"
  )

# 4. Merge the template with the transplant data AND the centroids
# We join the transplant data to the map template, and then add the calculated centroids.
world_map_with_data <- map_data_template %>%
  left_join(transplant_data %>% select(rnaturalearth_name, type, transplants),
            by = c("name_long" = "rnaturalearth_name", "type")) %>%
  # Fill any NA values in 'transplants' with 0 (for countries/types with no data)
  mutate(transplants = tidyr::replace_na(transplants, 0)) %>%
  # Finally, join the centroids to this combined data frame
  left_join(country_centroids, by = "name_long")

# --- Filter for non-zero transplant values for labeling ---
# We create a separate data frame specifically for the labels.
# This ensures that numbers are only plotted on countries that actually have transplants (> 0).
data_for_labels <- world_map_with_data %>%
  filter(transplants > 0)

# 5. Create the two faceted maps with non-overlapping numbers
# We use ggplot2 to create the map.
ggplot(data = world_map_with_data) +
  # Plot all countries as a base layer with a light grey fill and white borders.
  geom_sf(data=world_map_with_data, aes(fill = as.integer(transplants>0)), color = "black", size = 0.2) +
  # Add the transplant numbers using geom_text_repel for non-overlapping labels.
  # 'data = data_for_labels' ensures only non-zero values are labeled.
  # 'x = lon' and 'y = lat' are the original centroid positions.
  # 'label = transplants' displays the actual number.
  scale_fill_continuous(type='gradient',low = "white", high = "black") + 
  geom_text_repel(data = data_for_labels,
                  aes(x = lon, y = lat, label = transplants,size=log(transplants)),
                  color = "red", # Color of the numbers
                  #size = transplants,        # Size of the numbers
                  fontface = "bold", # Make numbers bold for better visibility
                  box.padding = 0.5, # Padding around the label box
                  point.padding = 0.5, # Padding around the point
                  min.segment.length = 0, # Always draw a segment if label is moved
                  max.overlaps = Inf, # Try to resolve all overlaps
                  force = 1, # Strength of repulsion
                  seed = 123 # For reproducible label placement
                  ) +
  # Use facet_wrap to create separate maps for "Deceased" and "Living" transplants.
  facet_wrap(~ type, ncol = 1) +

  # Polish the maps for a clean and informative look.
  labs(
    title = "Transplant Counts by Country",
    subtitle = "Numbers represent the total transplants for each type (Deceased or Living)"
  ) +
  theme_void() + # A minimalist theme without axes or grid lines
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(hjust = 0.5, face = "bold", size = 16), # Center and bold title
    plot.subtitle = element_text(hjust = 0.5), # Center subtitle
    legend.position = "none", # No legend is needed as the numbers are displayed directly
    strip.text = element_text(face = "bold", size = 12) # Bold facet titles
  )


```
